title: Track ID validation logic duplicated between verify.rs and domain ids.rs
description: The function `validate_track_id_str_local` in `apps/cli-composition/src/verify.rs` (lines 148–176) encodes exactly the same track ID grammar as `is_valid_track_id` in `libs/domain/src/ids.rs` (lines 232–252): same first-char guard (lowercase/digit), same inner char loop (lowercase/digit/hyphen), same double-hyphen rejection, same trailing-hyphen rejection. The two diverge only in output type (Result<(), String> vs bool) and error message phrasing. If the track ID grammar ever changes, both sites must be updated or they will silently diverge.

locations.summary: validate_track_id_str_local: hand-rolled track ID grammar check returning descriptive errors
is_valid_track_id: identical grammar implemented as a bool predicate, backing TrackId::try_new

rationale: Encodes a domain business rule (the exact character set and structural constraints for track IDs) in two places across architectural layers. A grammar change (e.g. allowing uppercase) would require updating both; a discrepancy would cause verify.rs to accept or reject IDs that TrackId itself would not.
suggested_fix: In `verify.rs`, call `domain::TrackId::try_new(value).map_err(|e| e.to_string()).map(|_| ())` instead of `validate_track_id_str_local`. Since `cli-composition` already has access to the domain crate (other methods use domain types indirectly via usecase), this eliminates the local copy while keeping the CN-02 public boundary clean.
verify_reason: Confirmed. `validate_track_id_str_local` (verify.rs:148-176) and `is_valid_track_id` (ids.rs:232-252) implement the same track ID grammar (first-char guard, inner char loop, double-hyphen rejection, trailing-hyphen rejection) in two separate files across architectural layers. The docstring comment in verify.rs even admits the duplication ('without importing domain types'). A grammar change (e.g. allowing uppercase) must be applied in both places or they silently diverge. Genuine knowledge-dup spanning layers.

make_track_pr_ensure: identical body except sotp subcommand is "ensure-pr"

rationale: Any change to the positional-track-id promotion guard must be made twice. The two functions are already composed in `make_track_pr` (line 401–408), showing the design anticipates them being called together — making a shared helper especially natural.
suggested_fix: Extract a private helper `fn build_pr_forward_args(subcommand: &str, raw_args: &[String]) -> Vec<String>` and call it from both methods.
verify_reason: Confirmed. `make_track_pr_push` (make.rs:351-370) and `make_track_pr_ensure` (make.rs:376-395) are structurally identical: same `strip_leading_separator` call, same `match words.first() { Some(first) if !first.starts_with("--") => ... }` guard, same `run_sotp` forward. Only the subcommand string literals differ ('push' vs 'ensure-pr'). Any guard logic change (e.g. supporting additional positional args) requires identical edits in both functions. Near-clone within the same file with a clear helper extraction opportunity.

title: `implemented_in` non-empty invariant enforced in two separate sites
description: The rule "implemented_in must not be empty" is encoded in both `AcceptedDecision::implement` (lines 83-85) and `ImplementedDecision::new` (lines 149-151). Both perform `if implemented_in.is_empty() { return Err(EmptyImplementedIn) }` before constructing an `ImplementedDecision`. A refinement to the validation (e.g., trimming or format checks) would require updating both sites.

locations.summary: AcceptedDecision::implement — validates implemented_in non-empty, constructs ImplementedDecision inline
ImplementedDecision::new — same non-empty guard, same construction logic

rationale: Two copies of the same domain invariant. `ImplementedDecision::new` is the canonical constructor; `AcceptedDecision::implement` could delegate to it rather than duplicating the guard and direct field construction.
suggested_fix: Replace the body of `AcceptedDecision::implement` with `ImplementedDecision::new(self.common, implemented_in)`, so the non-empty invariant is owned solely by `ImplementedDecision::new`.
verify_reason: Confirmed. AcceptedDecision::implement (lines 83-86) and ImplementedDecision::new (lines 149-152) both guard if implemented_in.is_empty() { return Err(EmptyImplementedIn); } before constructing an ImplementedDecision. The transition method could delegate to the constructor and remove the duplicated guard. Two copies of the same domain invariant, short and localized. Low severity is appropriate.

title: Identical trim-then-non-empty validation in NonEmptyString and ReviewGroupName
description: NonEmptyString::try_new and ReviewGroupName::try_new share a byte-identical single-line body: `let trimmed = value.into().trim().to_owned(); if trimmed.is_empty() { Err(ValidationError::EmptyString) } else { Ok(Self(trimmed)) }`. Both types represent the same invariant (trimmed, non-empty string) with the same error. The only difference is the wrapper type name. A single private helper function `try_trimmed_non_empty(value: impl Into<String>) -> Result<String, ValidationError>` would remove the duplication.

locations.summary: NonEmptyString::try_new — trim + empty check
ReviewGroupName::try_new — identical trim + empty check

rationale: Two copies of the same invariant (trim, reject empty) with the same error type. A future change to the validation logic (e.g., normalising Unicode whitespace) must be applied in both places or one copy silently diverges.
suggested_fix: Extract a private `fn validate_trimmed_non_empty(value: impl Into<String>) -> Result<String, ValidationError>` in ids.rs and call it from both try_new implementations.
verify_reason: Confirmed. NonEmptyString::try_new (ids.rs line 178-181) and ReviewGroupName::try_new (ids.rs line 214-217) are byte-identical single-expression bodies: let trimmed = value.into().trim().to_owned(); if trimmed.is_empty() { Err(ValidationError::EmptyString) } else { Ok(Self(trimmed)) }. Same validation rule, same error type, different wrapper type names only. A private helper function would remove the duplication. A future change to the validation (e.g., Unicode normalization) must be applied at both sites. Medium severity is appropriate.

title: Duplicate TrackStatus → TrackPhaseInfo match in resolve_phase and resolve_phase_from_record
description: resolve_phase (lines 82-131) and resolve_phase_from_record (lines 143-186) both contain a full match on all six TrackStatus variants, producing identical TrackPhaseInfo literals for Planned, InProgress, Done, and Archived. Only Blocked and Cancelled differ in how the override reason is extracted (from TrackMetadata vs from an Option<&str>). The arms for Planned, InProgress, Done, and Archived are byte-identical across both functions, including the hard-coded reason strings.

locations.summary: resolve_phase — full match on TrackStatus
resolve_phase_from_record — duplicate match on TrackStatus

rationale: Four of six match arms are exact duplicates including hard-coded reason strings. Adding a new TrackStatus variant (or changing a reason string) requires edits in both functions. resolve_phase can be a two-line wrapper that calls resolve_phase_from_record after extracting the override reason from TrackMetadata.
suggested_fix: Implement resolve_phase as: `let override_reason = track.status_override().map(|o| o.reason()); resolve_phase_from_record(derive_track_status(impl_plan, track.status_override()), override_reason)`, then remove the duplicated match arms.
verify_reason: Confirmed. resolve_phase (lines 75-132) and resolve_phase_from_record (lines 139-187) both match on all six TrackStatus variants. The Planned, InProgress, Done, and Archived arms are byte-identical including hard-coded reason strings. Only Blocked and Cancelled differ in how override_reason is extracted (track.status_override().map(|o| o.reason()) vs a passed Option<&str>). Adding a new TrackStatus variant or changing a reason string requires edits in both functions. resolve_phase can trivially delegate to resolve_phase_from_record after extracting the override reason. Medium severity is correct.

title: is_branch_delete and is_checkout_branch_create share an identical git-option-skipping preamble
description: Both `is_branch_delete` and `is_checkout_branch_create` open with a structurally identical while-loop that skips git global options (using `GIT_OPTIONS_WITH_ARG`) and `--`, differing only in the subcommand name they match ("branch" vs "checkout") and the boolean flag they set. This preamble duplicates the same traversal already present in `extract_git_subcommand`. A change to how git global options are skipped (e.g., adding a new entry to `GIT_OPTIONS_WITH_ARG` that the skip loop must handle differently) would require edits in all three functions.

locations.summary: is_branch_delete: git-option-skipping preamble (lines 307-330) then flag scan for -d/-D/--delete
is_checkout_branch_create: near-identical git-option-skipping preamble (lines 360-382) then flag scan for -b/-B/--orphan
extract_git_subcommand: the original git-option-skipping loop that both functions duplicate

rationale: The git-global-option skipping loop (checking `GIT_OPTIONS_WITH_ARG`, `--` sentinel, and `starts_with('-')`) is written three times. The two checker functions could instead call `extract_git_subcommand` to obtain the subcommand index and then proceed with their specific flag scan, eliminating the duplicated traversal.
suggested_fix: Refactor `is_branch_delete` and `is_checkout_branch_create` to reuse `extract_git_subcommand` for finding the subcommand position, then start their flag-scan from the index returned. Alternatively, introduce a helper `find_git_subcommand_index(argv, git_index) -> Option<usize>` that returns the index of the subcommand token, and use it in all three callsites.
verify_reason: Confirmed. Lines 305-354 (is_branch_delete) and 358-405 (is_checkout_branch_create) each contain a structurally identical while-loop that skips git global options using GIT_OPTIONS_WITH_ARG, '--', and starts_with('-'), differing only in the subcommand string matched ('branch' vs 'checkout') and the boolean flag set. This loop is distinct from extract_git_subcommand (277-302) because it also sets found_branch/found_checkout and breaks differently, but the traversal logic is nearly byte-for-byte identical across both checker functions. Adding a new entry to GIT_OPTIONS_WITH_ARG that requires special handling would require edits in all three sites. This is a genuine near-clone with change-amplification risk. Medium severity is appropriate for 2 copies of ~20 lines of non-trivial parsing logic.

title: MAX_NESTING_DEPTH constant defined independently in domain (text.rs) and infrastructure (conch.rs)
description: The constant `MAX_NESTING_DEPTH: usize = 16` is defined once in `libs/domain/src/guard/text.rs` (for the hand-written `extract_substitutions_inner`) and again independently in `libs/infrastructure/src/shell/conch.rs` (for `split_shell_inner` and `collect_from_top_level_command`). Both enforce the same conceptual limit on command-substitution nesting depth. If the limit must be changed, both definitions must be updated in sync.

locations.summary: const MAX_NESTING_DEPTH: usize = 16; in the domain guard text module
const MAX_NESTING_DEPTH: usize = 16; in the conch-parser infrastructure adapter

rationale: Two independent magic-number definitions for the same policy limit. A change to one without the other silently creates inconsistent behavior between the hand-written parser path and the conch-parser path.
suggested_fix: Expose `MAX_NESTING_DEPTH` as a `pub(crate)` or `pub` constant from the domain guard module (e.g., `domain::guard::MAX_NESTING_DEPTH`) and import it in the infrastructure crate, or move it to a shared constants module that both layers can reference.
verify_reason: Confirmed. MAX_NESTING_DEPTH: usize = 16 appears at text.rs:13 and conch.rs:25, both enforcing the same conceptual limit on command-substitution nesting depth. These are in different crates (domain vs infrastructure), meaning this is a data-dup of a policy constant that would require synchronized updates if the limit changes. However, since these two parsers are intentionally separate (hand-written path vs conch-parser path), the separation is partly justified by hexagonal architecture. The risk is real but modest — a silent inconsistency if one is changed without the other. Low severity is appropriate.

title: AdrAnchor and ConventionAnchor are near-identical non-empty string newtypes
description: AdrAnchor (adr_ref.rs:13-38) and ConventionAnchor (convention_ref.rs:12-41) are structurally identical: both wrap a String, validate non-empty via `value.trim().is_empty()`, and implement `AsRef<str>` and `fmt::Display` with byte-for-byte identical bodies. The only differences are the type name and the ValidationError variant returned. Any future change to the anchor validation rule (e.g., adding a max-length or character-set constraint) must be applied in two places.

locations.summary: AdrAnchor newtype: try_new (non-empty check), AsRef<str>, Display — all delegating to self.0
ConventionAnchor newtype: identical structure, identical validation rule, identical trait impls — only type name and error variant differ

rationale: Both types encode the same invariant ('anchor string must be non-empty') and have identical trait-impl bodies. A single conceptual change to the anchor validation policy forces edits in two files. The duplication is 8+ lines of structurally identical logic.
suggested_fix: Introduce a private macro (e.g., `macro_rules! non_empty_string_newtype!`) or a generic `NonEmptyStr<E>` newtype parameterised on the error variant, and derive both AdrAnchor and ConventionAnchor from it. Given the ADR explicitly notes that strict validation is deferred to Q15, a shared base is the natural extraction point.
verify_reason: Confirmed. AdrAnchor (adr_ref.rs:13-38) and ConventionAnchor (convention_ref.rs:12-41) are near-identical: both wrap a String, validate with value.trim().is_empty(), implement AsRef<str> and fmt::Display with identical bodies. The only differences are the type name and the ValidationError variant (EmptyAdrAnchor vs EmptyConventionAnchor). The validation invariant ('anchor must be non-empty') is the same knowledge encoded twice. Adding a max-length or character-set constraint would require edits in both files. These are not separated by hexagonal layer boundaries — both are in the same domain plan_ref module. Medium severity is appropriate: 8+ lines of structurally identical logic encoding the same validation rule.

title: Verdict and FastVerdict are structurally identical enums with identical constructor bodies
description: Both `Verdict` and `FastVerdict` declare the same two variants (`ZeroFindings`, `FindingsRemain(NonEmptyReviewerFindings)`) and each provides an identical `findings_remain(findings: Vec<ReviewerFinding>) -> Result<Self, VerdictError>` method whose body is byte-for-byte the same: `Ok(Self::FindingsRemain(NonEmptyReviewerFindings::new(findings)?))`. Any change to the `findings_remain` constructor logic (e.g., sorting, deduplication, a new validation step) must be applied in both places, creating silent divergence risk.

locations.summary: Verdict enum definition and its findings_remain constructor
FastVerdict enum definition — structurally identical to Verdict, with byte-identical findings_remain constructor body

rationale: Two copies of non-trivial constructor logic. The distinct types are intentional (type-safety to prevent misuse of fast vs. final verdicts), but the constructor body is not type-specific and could be extracted into a helper on `NonEmptyReviewerFindings` or factored via a shared trait/macro to eliminate the duplication while retaining the distinct types.
suggested_fix: Extract the shared constructor logic into a private helper, e.g. `fn make_findings_remain<T, F>(findings: Vec<ReviewerFinding>, wrap: F) -> Result<T, VerdictError> where F: FnOnce(NonEmptyReviewerFindings) -> T`. Both `Verdict::findings_remain` and `FastVerdict::findings_remain` delegate to it. Alternatively, implement a private `VerdictLike` trait with a blanket impl and a blanket `findings_remain` method.
verify_reason: Confirmed. Verdict (types.rs:296-310) and FastVerdict (types.rs:325-339) each contain a findings_remain method whose body is byte-for-byte identical: Ok(Self::FindingsRemain(NonEmptyReviewerFindings::new(findings)?)). The comment in the source acknowledges they are 'same structure as Verdict but distinct type.' While the distinct types are intentional for type safety, the one-line constructor body could be extracted to NonEmptyReviewerFindings. However, this is a single-line trivial delegation — effectively a one-liner constructor that is idiomatic for newtype enums in Rust. The change-amplification risk is real but minimal for a 1-line body. Adjusting severity to low (not medium) because the actual duplicated logic is a single expression, well below the ~8-line threshold for medium.

title: Expand-then-compile-glob pattern repeated three times in ReviewScopeConfig::new
description: Inside `ReviewScopeConfig::new` (scope_config.rs lines 66-99), three consecutive blocks each perform the same operation: iterate a `Vec<String>` of patterns, call an expand function, call `compile_glob`, map the error to a specific `ScopeConfigError` variant carrying `{ pattern, source }`, and collect into `Result<Vec<GlobMatcher>, _>`. The only variation is (a) whether `expand_track_id` or `expand_other_track` is called, and (b) which `ScopeConfigError` variant wraps the failure. All three blocks share the identical collect turbofish `collect::<Result<Vec<_>, _>>()` and the expand/compile sequence.

locations.summary: Named scope patterns: expand_track_id → compile_glob → ScopeConfigError::InvalidPattern
Operational patterns: expand_track_id → compile_glob → ScopeConfigError::InvalidOperationalPattern
Other-track patterns: expand_other_track → compile_glob → ScopeConfigError::InvalidOtherTrackPattern

rationale: Three structurally-identical iterator chains that differ only in expander function and error variant. A small private helper `compile_patterns<F, E>(patterns: &[String], expand: F, wrap_err: E) -> Result<Vec<GlobMatcher>, ScopeConfigError>` would collapse all three into single call-sites and make future changes (e.g. enabling `literal_separator`) apply uniformly.
suggested_fix: Extract a private function: `fn compile_patterns(patterns: &[String], expand: impl Fn(&str) -> String, wrap_err: impl Fn(String, globset::Error) -> ScopeConfigError) -> Result<Vec<GlobMatcher>, ScopeConfigError>`. Each of the three call-sites passes its own expand closure and error-constructor closure.
verify_reason: Confirmed. ReviewScopeConfig::new (scope_config.rs:66-99) contains three structurally identical iterator chains that each: iterate a Vec<String>, call an expand function, call compile_glob, map the error to a ScopeConfigError variant with { pattern, source }, and collect into Result<Vec<_>, _>. The chains differ only in the expand function (expand_track_id vs expand_other_track) and the error variant. A helper function would eliminate the duplication. However, the first block is inside a for loop with additional logic (scope_name construction, scopes.insert) and uses .iter().map() on a borrowed slice differently from the outer two. The structural repetition is real but the blocks are short (~8-9 lines each) and the abstraction gain is modest. Low severity is appropriate.

title: Duplicated `is_valid_rust_identifier` with divergent semantics
description: Two private functions with the same name `is_valid_rust_identifier` implement Rust identifier validation in different modules. The body in `catalogue.rs` adds a bare-underscore rejection (`if s == "_" { return false; }`) that is absent from the otherwise identical body in `identifiers.rs`. The shared character-class logic (ASCII alpha/underscore for first char, ASCII alphanumeric/underscore for the rest) is duplicated verbatim. This creates a silent behavioural discrepancy: `"_"` is valid per `identifiers.rs` but invalid per `catalogue.rs`.

locations.summary: Rejects bare `"_"` in addition to the shared ASCII-identifier rules
Same ASCII-identifier body but does NOT reject bare `"_"`

rationale: The duplicated logic encodes the same domain knowledge (what counts as a valid Rust identifier) in two places. The semantics diverge silently, making it easy for future edits to the two files to drift further apart. A shared crate-internal helper (or a re-export from `identifiers`) should encode the invariant once.
suggested_fix: Extract a single private `is_valid_rust_identifier(s: &str, reject_bare_underscore: bool) -> bool` (or two named helpers) into `catalogue_v2::identifiers` and have `catalogue.rs` call it with the underscore-rejection flag set to `true`.
verify_reason: Confirmed: two private `is_valid_rust_identifier` functions exist — one in `catalogue.rs` (lines 49-61) and one in `identifiers.rs` (lines 58-67). The character-class logic is verbatim identical but `catalogue.rs` adds a bare-underscore rejection that `identifiers.rs` lacks, creating a silent semantic divergence. Both encode the same domain knowledge (valid Rust identifier definition). A single shared helper would eliminate the divergence risk. Medium severity is correct: 2 copies, the divergence can cause correctness bugs if one site is updated.

title: Structurally identical `WriterError` enums for baseline-graph and contract-map ports
description: `BaselineGraphWriterError` and `ContractMapWriterError` are declared in different modules but are structurally identical: both have exactly three named-field variants `IoError { path: PathBuf, reason: String }`, `SymlinkRejected { path: PathBuf }`, and `TrackNotFound { track_id, expected_dir: PathBuf }`. Their Display implementations produce the same format strings, differing only in that `BaselineGraphWriterError::TrackNotFound.track_id` is typed `TrackId` (a newtype) while `ContractMapWriterError::TrackNotFound.track_id` is a raw `String`. The doc comment on `BaselineGraphWriterError` explicitly states it is "Symmetric to `ContractMapWriterError`".

locations.summary: `BaselineGraphWriterError` — 3 variants + Display + Error impl
`ContractMapWriterError` — same 3 variants via `thiserror::Error`

rationale: Both error types encode the same three failure modes (I/O, symlink rejection, missing track directory) with identical field shapes and message formats. Any new failure mode or field rename must be applied twice. A shared type (or a shared macro) would eliminate the duplication while preserving separate public names if needed.
suggested_fix: Define a single parameterised error type (e.g. `WriterError<TrackId>`) in a shared module, or use `thiserror` with a common base. If the public API must expose distinct names, type-alias or wrap the common type.
verify_reason: Confirmed: `BaselineGraphWriterError` (baseline_graph_ports.rs lines 187-231, manual Display impl) and `ContractMapWriterError` (catalogue_ports.rs lines 108-123, thiserror derive) share the same three variants with identical field shapes and identical message formats. The only differences are `TrackId` vs `String` for track_id, and the error derivation style. These are two separate port error types — not a domain-vs-adapter separation — and the doc comment explicitly calls them 'Symmetric'. A new failure mode must be added twice. Medium severity is warranted: 2 copies, and the implementation styles already diverged (manual Display vs thiserror), demonstrating drift.

title: Structurally identical `RendererError` enums for baseline-graph and contract-map ports
description: `BaselineGraphRendererError` and `ContractMapRendererError` share an identical three-variant structure: `StyleConfigNotFound { path: PathBuf }`, `StyleConfigInvalid { path: PathBuf, reason: String }`, `RenderFailed { reason: String }`. Their Display implementations follow the same `"<context> style configuration not found: {path}"` / `"<context> style configuration is invalid at {path}: {reason}"` / `"<context> rendering failed: {reason}"` patterns, with only the context prefix differing (`"baseline-graph"` vs `"contract-map"`). The doc comment on `BaselineGraphRendererError` explicitly states it is "Symmetric to `ContractMapRendererError`".

locations.summary: `BaselineGraphRendererError` — 3 variants + Display + Error impl
`ContractMapRendererError` — same 3 variants + Display + Error impl

rationale: Both enums model the same three renderer failure modes with the same field shapes. The only variation is a context label in the Display strings. Adding a new variant or field requires editing two enums and two Display impls in sync.
suggested_fix: Introduce a shared `RendererError` type parameterised on a context label string (compile-time or runtime), and re-export under the existing public names. Alternatively, unify the two enums into a single `RenderError { context: &'static str, kind: RenderErrorKind }` style.
verify_reason: Confirmed: `BaselineGraphRendererError` (baseline_graph_ports.rs lines 130-174) and `ContractMapRendererError` (contract_map_renderer.rs lines 62-96) have identical three-variant structures with the same field shapes; only the context prefix string in Display differs ('baseline-graph' vs 'contract-map'). The doc comment explicitly flags them as symmetric. However, these are two distinct hexagonal port error types for separate rendering concerns; their separation is partially intentional design. The duplication is real but the abstraction opportunity (a parameterised enum or macro) is narrow. Severity adjusted down to low: 2 copies, the only variation is a string label, and the duplication is a deliberate symmetric design choice with low divergence risk in practice.

title: Three identical single-string error newtypes in `catalogue_impl_signals_ports.rs`
description: Three public structs in the same file — `BaselineCaptureIoError(pub String)`, `TrackStatusReadError(pub String)`, and `TypeSignalsExecutionError(pub String)` — are byte-for-byte identical in structure, Display impl (`write!(f, "{}", self.0)`), and `std::error::Error` impl (empty body). Only the type name and the surrounding trait differ.

locations.summary: `BaselineCaptureIoError(pub String)` with Display + Error impl
`TrackStatusReadError(pub String)` with Display + Error impl
`TypeSignalsExecutionError(pub String)` with Display + Error impl

rationale: All three newtypes provide no more structure than a raw `String`. Repeating the same three-line boilerplate three times in the same file adds noise without adding safety. A macro or a shared trait implementation would halve the line count.
suggested_fix: Define a crate-internal macro `string_error_newtype!(Name)` that emits the struct + Display + Error impls. Alternatively, use `thiserror` with `#[error("{0}")]` on a tuple-struct variant, which eliminates the manual Display impl. If the three types must remain distinct newtypes (for type safety at call sites), the macro approach is preferable.
verify_reason: Confirmed: `BaselineCaptureIoError` (lines 335-343), `TrackStatusReadError` (lines 388-396), and `TypeSignalsExecutionError` (lines 426-434) in the same file are byte-for-byte identical in structure — each is a `pub struct Foo(pub String)` with `Display` writing `self.0` and an empty `Error` impl. Three copies of the same 9-line boilerplate in the same file. A simple macro (e.g., `macro_rules! string_error_newtype!`) or a blanket helper would consolidate them. Low severity is correct: purely structural boilerplate, no logic divergence risk.

title: Sorting logic duplicated across `TypeBaselineEntry::new` and `TypeBaselineEntry::with_trait_impls`
description: `TypeBaselineEntry::new` (lines 132-133) and `TypeBaselineEntry::with_trait_impls` (lines 145-146) both contain the same two lines: `members.sort_by(|a, b| a.name().cmp(b.name()));` and `methods.sort_by(|a, b| a.name.cmp(&b.name));`. The only difference between the two constructors is that `with_trait_impls` additionally accepts a `trait_impls` parameter; the sorting step is copy-pasted verbatim.

locations.summary: `TypeBaselineEntry::new` — sorts members and methods before constructing Self
`TypeBaselineEntry::with_trait_impls` — identical sort before constructing Self

rationale: The sort order is a structural invariant of `TypeBaselineEntry`. Encoding it in two constructors means a future change to the sort key must be applied twice. The canonical fix is for `with_trait_impls` to call `new(kind, members, methods)` and then set `trait_impls`, or to factor the sort into a private `fn sort_members_methods(members: &mut Vec<MemberDeclaration>, methods: &mut Vec<MethodDeclaration>)`.
suggested_fix: Implement `with_trait_impls` as `let mut entry = Self::new(kind, members, methods); entry.trait_impls = trait_impls; entry` — or extract a private sort helper and call it from both constructors.
verify_reason: Confirmed: `TypeBaselineEntry::new` (lines 132-133) and `with_trait_impls` (lines 145-146) both contain the identical two sort lines verbatim. The sort order is a structural invariant; `with_trait_impls` could simply call `new(kind, members, methods)` and then set `trait_impls`. Only 2 lines duplicated, same file, simple fix. Low severity is correct.

title: Identical match-arm structure for `implemented` and `superseded` status dispatch
description: The `'implemented'` and `'superseded'` arms in `decision_dto_to_entry` are structurally identical: each unwraps an `Option<String>` field with the same `ok_or_else` + `InvalidDecisionField` pattern, calls a domain constructor, wraps the Ok with the corresponding `AdrDecisionEntry` variant, and maps the Err through the same `InvalidDecisionField(format!("decision '{id}': {e}"))` closure. Only the field name, status string, domain type, and variant differ.

locations.summary: `'implemented'` arm: unwraps `implemented_in`, calls `ImplementedDecision::new`, maps Ok to `AdrDecisionEntry::ImplementedDecision`, maps Err to `InvalidDecisionField`
`'superseded'` arm: unwraps `superseded_by`, calls `SupersededDecision::new`, maps Ok to `AdrDecisionEntry::SupersededDecision`, maps Err to `InvalidDecisionField` — byte-for-byte identical structure

rationale: Both arms are ~11 lines and share the same logic template. A single generic helper `fn build_decision_entry<T, F>(field: Option<String>, status: &str, field_name: &str, id: &str, ctor: F) -> Result<AdrDecisionEntry, AdrFrontMatterCodecError>` (or equivalent closure) would eliminate the duplication. Divergence risk: if the error format string or `map_err` pattern changes, only one arm may be updated.
suggested_fix: Extract a private helper that takes the `Option<String>` field, the required-status label, the id, and a constructor closure, then reuse it for both `implemented` and `superseded` arms.
verify_reason: Confirmed: the `'implemented'` arm (parse.rs lines 156-167) and `'superseded'` arm (lines 168-179) are structurally near-identical: each unwraps an `Option<String>` field with `ok_or_else` + `InvalidDecisionField`, calls a domain constructor, wraps the Ok with the corresponding variant, and maps the Err with the same format string pattern. Only the field name, required-field label, domain type, and variant differ. ~11 lines each. A generic helper or closure-based abstraction would consolidate them. Medium severity is correct: 2 copies of non-trivial error-wrapping logic; an error-format change must be applied twice.

title: Repeated `AdrFilePortError::ListPaths` error-mapping closure in `list_adr_paths`
description: Inside `list_adr_paths`, the closure `|e| AdrFilePortError::ListPaths(format!("{}: {e}", self.adr_dir.display()))` is written out three times (for `read_dir`, per-`entry` iteration, and `file_type` access), instead of being captured once in a local binding or a `fn list_err` helper.

locations.summary: `read_dir(…).map_err(|e| AdrFilePortError::ListPaths(format!(…)))` — first occurrence
`entry.map_err(|e| AdrFilePortError::ListPaths(format!(…)))` inside the for-loop — second occurrence
`entry.file_type().map_err(|e| AdrFilePortError::ListPaths(format!(…)))` — third occurrence

rationale: Three identical closures map I/O errors to the same `ListPaths` variant with the same format string. Changing the error message or variant requires editing three sites. A one-line local closure `let list_err = |e| AdrFilePortError::ListPaths(format!(…));` would consolidate them.
suggested_fix: Bind the closure once: `let list_err = |e: std::io::Error| AdrFilePortError::ListPaths(format!("{}: {e}", self.adr_dir.display()));` and pass `list_err` to all three `.map_err` calls.
verify_reason: Confirmed: the closure `|e| AdrFilePortError::ListPaths(format!("{}: {e}", self.adr_dir.display()))` appears three times on lines 39, 43-45, and 46-48 within `list_adr_paths`. A one-line local binding would consolidate them. This is minor local repetition within a single function — low severity is correct and the fix is trivial.

title: Four paginated GitHub API functions share identical body structure
description: list_reactions_with, list_reviews_with, list_issue_comments_with, and list_review_comments_with (gh_cli.rs) are structurally near-identical: each computes an endpoint URL, calls run_gh(&["api", &endpoint, "--paginate"]), and returns Ok(stdout) or Err(CommandFailed{command: format!("api {endpoint}"), stderr}). The only variation is the endpoint path template and the number of input parameters. A change to error formatting or the paginate flag would require updating all four.

locations.summary: list_reactions_with: repos/{repo_nwo}/issues/{pr}/reactions endpoint
list_reviews_with: repos/{repo_nwo}/pulls/{pr}/reviews endpoint
list_issue_comments_with: repos/{repo_nwo}/issues/{pr}/comments endpoint
list_review_comments_with: repos/{repo_nwo}/pulls/{pr}/reviews/{review_id}/comments endpoint

rationale: Four copies of the same 8-line paginated-API call pattern. Any change to error representation or paginate behavior would require four separate edits. A helper fn like `gh_api_paginate(endpoint, run_gh) -> Result<String, GhError>` would consolidate to a single site.
suggested_fix: Extract a private helper `fn gh_api_paginate_with<F>(endpoint: &str, run_gh: &F) -> Result<String, GhError>` that does the call and error handling, then have each list_*_with build the endpoint URL and delegate to it.

Only the endpoint URL template and parameter count differ. Four copies with the same 8-line body. Any change to error formatting or paginate behavior requires four edits. title: Test git() helper function cloned in show.rs and merge_gate_adapter.rs

title: build_full_prompt duplicated verbatim in ClaudeReviewer and CodexReviewer

title: Four verdict-conversion helpers duplicated across claude_reviewer and codex_reviewer

convert_raw_to_final (172-186), convert_raw_to_fast (192-206), require_successful_payload (209-224), convert_findings_to_domain (227-243)

title: Timestamped unique runtime-path generation duplicated in codex_reviewer and review_fix_runner/spawn
description: The algorithm for generating a unique, timestamped path under `tmp/reviewer-runtime` is implemented twice: as `runtime_path` in `codex_reviewer.rs` and as `fixer_runtime_path` in `review_fix_runner/spawn.rs`. The only difference is the error type returned (`String` vs `ReviewFixRunnerError`).

fn fixer_runtime_path — identical algorithm, returns Result<PathBuf, ReviewFixRunnerError>

If the algorithm needs to change — e.g., to use UUID, or to switch from nanos to a monotonic clock — both copies must be updated. The `REVIEW_RUNTIME_DIR` constant is also duplicated (see separate finding). An error in one copy but not the other (e.g., forgetting `create_dir_all`) would silently cause failures in only one code path.
suggested_fix: Extract a shared `review_runtime_path(prefix, ext) -> Result<PathBuf, String>` helper into a common module (e.g. `review_v2::runtime`). `fixer_runtime_path` maps the `String` error to `ReviewFixRunnerError::Unexpected`.
verify_reason: Confirmed: runtime_path in codex_reviewer.rs (lines 249-267) and fixer_runtime_path in spawn.rs (lines 14-33) implement the same algorithm (static AtomicU64 counter + SystemTime nanoseconds + process ID → join into path string, create_dir_all on parent). The only difference is the error type returned (String vs ReviewFixRunnerError). The uniqueness algorithm is a single piece of knowledge that must be kept in sync.

title: Async-bridge OS-thread spawn logic duplicated between `new` and `block_on_thread`
description: In `index.rs`, the `new` constructor (lines 107–117) manually performs the same "spawn OS thread → handle.block_on(future) → join → map errors" bridging pattern that the `block_on_thread` generic helper (lines 159–179) encapsulates. The `new` constructor cannot call `self.block_on_thread` because `self` does not yet exist, but the knowledge of _how_ to safely drive an async future on a dedicated OS thread is expressed twice. A free function `fn block_on_with_handle<F, T, E>(handle: Handle, future: F, thread_err: E) -> Result<T, E>` would serve both call sites and eliminate the duplication.

locations.summary: Inline async-bridge: thread::Builder::new().name("lancedb-connect").spawn(|| handle.block_on(...)).map_err(spawn_err)?.join().map_err(panic_err)?.map_err(lancedb_err)
`block_on_thread` generic method: same thread::Builder::new().spawn(|| handle.block_on(future)) → join() → unwrap_or(Err(thread_err)) pattern

rationale: A single conceptual change to how OS-thread bridging works (e.g. adding a timeout, changing thread naming, or altering panic handling) requires edits in two places. The duplication spans ~10 lines of non-trivial async-bridging logic and would cause divergence if either copy is updated without the other.
suggested_fix: Extract a free function `fn block_on_with_handle<F, T, E>(handle: tokio::runtime::Handle, future: F, thread_err: E) -> Result<T, E>` that performs the spawn+join+error-map sequence. Call it from both `new` (passing `runtime.handle().clone()`) and from `block_on_thread` (passing `self.runtime.as_ref().map(|rt| rt.handle().clone())`). This makes `block_on_thread` a thin wrapper around the free function and eliminates the inline copy in `new`.
verify_reason: Confirmed: the OS-thread async-bridge pattern in new (lines 107-117) and block_on_thread (lines 159-179) encodes the same 'spawn OS thread → block_on → join → map errors' knowledge. The constructor cannot call self.block_on_thread because self does not yet exist, but a free function fn block_on_with_handle(handle: Handle, future: F, thread_err: E) would serve both call sites. The duplication is approximately 10 lines of non-trivial logic; any change to threading strategy (timeout, thread naming, panic handling) requires two edits. Medium severity is correct.

title: Repeated column-fetch + type-downcast boilerplate in `extract_similar_fragments`

First instance: COL_PATH column fetch and downcast to StringArray

suggested_fix: Introduce a local macro or closure `get_col_as!(batch, NAME, TYPE, ERROR_VARIANT)` that performs column_by_name + downcast_ref in one expression, used consistently for all three column accesses.

The cited line ranges are imprecise (one sub-range is a subset of the other), but the structural-dup is real. title: Duplicate nesting-depth guard in split_shell_inner and collect_from_top_level_command
description: The identical `if depth > MAX_NESTING_DEPTH { return Err(ParseError::NestingDepthExceeded { max: MAX_NESTING_DEPTH }) }` guard appears at both the entry of `split_shell_inner` (which starts the traversal at depth 0) and at the entry of `collect_from_top_level_command` (which is called from inside `split_shell_inner` at the same depth). Because `split_shell_inner` already performs the check before calling `collect_from_top_level_command`, the second check encodes the same guard rule in two places. If the guard semantics change (e.g. switching from `>` to `>=`, or adding a different error variant), both sites must be updated.

locations.summary: split_shell_inner: `if depth > MAX_NESTING_DEPTH { return Err(ParseError::NestingDepthExceeded { max: MAX_NESTING_DEPTH }); }`
collect_from_top_level_command: identical guard duplicated

rationale: Two copies of the same guard condition in the same file; a change to the depth-limit rule (e.g. the error variant or the comparison operator) must be applied to both sites.
suggested_fix: Remove the guard from `collect_from_top_level_command`. The entry-point guard in `split_shell_inner` is sufficient because the initial depth is 0 and every recursive call increments depth before calling `collect_from_top_level_command` again through `split_shell_inner` (or through direct `depth + 1` increments whose callers already carry the accumulated depth).
verify_reason: Confirmed: lines 105-108 (split_shell_inner) and 134-136 (collect_from_top_level_command) both contain the identical `if depth > MAX_NESTING_DEPTH { return Err(ParseError::NestingDepthExceeded { max: MAX_NESTING_DEPTH }) }` guard. Since split_shell_inner always checks before calling collect_from_top_level_command with the same depth value, the second check is redundant and encodes the same guard rule twice. A change to the comparison operator or error variant must be applied in both places. Severity is low: only 3 lines, same file, trivially fixed by removing the guard from the inner function or adding a comment explaining its defensive purpose.

title: Parallel AST-traversal stacks for CommandList → ListableCommand → PipeableCommand in conch.rs and flatten.rs
description: Both `conch.rs` and `flatten.rs` contain a three-level function stack that encodes the same structural knowledge of the conch-parser AST: how to descend through `CommandList` (iterate `.first` then `.rest`, matching `AndOr::And | AndOr::Or`), then through `ListableCommand` (match `Single` / `Pipe`), then through `PipeableCommand` (match `Simple` / `Compound` / `FunctionDef`). The only difference is what each stack does at the leaf: `conch.rs` recursively collects `SimpleCommand` values; `flatten.rs` calls a `collect` closure to extract token strings. A change to how conch models these nodes (e.g. a new `AndOr` variant) must be applied to both stacks.

locations.summary: collect_from_and_or_list / collect_from_listable / collect_from_pipeable — collects SimpleCommands with depth tracking
walk_and_or_for_flatten / walk_listable_for_flatten / walk_pipeable_for_flatten — same three-level descent with a closure leaf

rationale: The three-level traversal structure is the same knowledge (how the conch AST is shaped at the list/pipeline/pipeable level). Divergence would occur if a new AST node type is added to one walker but not the other. A generic visitor or a shared traversal helper parameterised on the leaf action would eliminate the duplication.
suggested_fix: Extract a generic `walk_command_list<F>(list, f)` visitor (where `F` is called on each `SimpleCommand`) and use it from both `collect_from_and_or_list` and `walk_and_or_for_flatten`. The `depth`-tracking argument is only needed for the collecting variant and can remain local to `conch.rs`.
verify_reason: Confirmed: conch.rs lines 146-204 and flatten.rs lines 138-197 both implement a three-level AST traversal through CommandList → AndOrList → ListableCommand (Single/Pipe) → PipeableCommand (Simple/Compound/FunctionDef). The structural knowledge (AST shape and how to descend it) is duplicated. They differ only in leaf action: conch.rs collects SimpleCommands recursively; flatten.rs calls a closure. A new AndOr or PipeableCommand variant would require updates in both files. Medium severity is appropriate: two files, ~50 lines each, spanning the same module but different files; a shared AST visitor parameterised on the leaf action would eliminate the duplication.

title: Parallel word-tree traversal in flatten.rs: flatten_complex_word/flatten_word vs collect_subst_from_complex_word/collect_subst_from_word
description: Inside `flatten.rs` there are two parallel two-level function stacks that both encode the same structural descent through the conch word AST: `ComplexWord::Single(w)` → delegate to word handler; `ComplexWord::Concat(words)` → iterate and delegate; `Word::Simple(sw)` → delegate to simple-word handler; `Word::SingleQuoted` → leaf; `Word::DoubleQuoted(parts)` → iterate and delegate. The first stack (`flatten_complex_word` / `flatten_word`, lines 19–58) produces a `String`. The second stack (`collect_subst_from_complex_word` / `collect_subst_from_word`, lines 242–266) collects command substitutions into a `Vec`. The structural traversal knowledge is duplicated; adding a new `Word` or `ComplexWord` variant requires updates in both stacks.

locations.summary: flatten_complex_word / flatten_word — descend ComplexWord → Word → SimpleWord to produce a String
collect_subst_from_complex_word / collect_subst_from_word — identical descent through ComplexWord → Word to collect substitutions

rationale: Both stacks mirror the same word-tree shape. A new word variant (e.g. a hypothetical `Word::Backtick`) or a structural change in `ComplexWord` would require symmetric edits in both stacks. The duplication is purely structural — the leaf actions differ — so a generic fold/visitor over the word tree would eliminate it.
suggested_fix: Introduce a generic `visit_word<F>(word, f)` / `visit_complex_word<F>(cw, f)` pair where `F` receives each `SimpleWord`. Both `flatten_word` and `collect_subst_from_word` become callers of this visitor with different `F` implementations.
verify_reason: Confirmed: flatten.rs contains two parallel word-tree traversal stacks. Lines 19-58 (flatten_complex_word/flatten_word) and lines 242-266 (collect_subst_from_complex_word/collect_subst_from_word) both mirror the same ComplexWord::Single/Concat → Word::Simple/SingleQuoted/DoubleQuoted descent. A new ComplexWord or Word variant would require symmetric edits in both stacks. The leaf actions differ (String vs Vec collection), but the traversal structure is duplicated. Medium severity is appropriate: within a single file, ~25-40 lines each, clear abstraction opportunity via a generic fold.

title: InformalGroundRefDto and informal_ground_kind_from_str duplicated between spec/codec.rs and tddd/spec_ground_codec.rs
description: The `InformalGroundRefDto` struct (a `{kind: String, summary: String}` DTO with `deny_unknown_fields`) and the `informal_ground_kind_from_str` function (a match over the four `InformalGroundKind` variants) are independently defined in both `libs/infrastructure/src/spec/codec.rs` and `libs/infrastructure/src/tddd/spec_ground_codec.rs`. Private `informal_ground_kind_from_str(s: &str) -> Option<InformalGroundKind>` mapping the same four string literals to the same enum variants

suggested_fix: In `spec/codec.rs`, remove the private `InformalGroundRefDto` struct and `informal_ground_kind_from_str` function. Import `InformalGroundRefDto` from `crate::tddd::spec_ground_codec` and delegate `informal_ground_ref_from_dto` / `informal_ground_ref_to_dto` to the `informal_grounds_from_dtos` / `informal_grounds_to_dtos` helpers already provided by that module.
verify_reason: Confirmed: InformalGroundRefDto struct is defined independently in both spec/codec.rs (lines 127-132) and tddd/spec_ground_codec.rs (lines 56-63). The informal_ground_kind_from_str function appears in both files: spec/codec.rs lines 313-323 (returns Result<InformalGroundKind, String>) and tddd/spec_ground_codec.rs lines 135-143 (returns Option<InformalGroundKind>). The tddd module exists explicitly to centralise this wire-format knowledge, yet spec/codec.rs carries its own private copy. The return type divergence confirms the copies have already started drifting. Any new InformalGroundKind variant must be added in both files. Medium severity: cross-file duplication of a mapping table and a DTO struct with a clear intent to centralise.

title: Baseline capture symlink guard + entire capture sequence duplicated across two adapters

title: validate_track_id slug-validation function triplicated across usecase modules
description: A ~38-line `validate_track_id` function implementing the lowercase-slug grammar `[a-z0-9]([a-z0-9-]*[a-z0-9])?` (empty check, first-char check, per-char loop with double-hyphen guard, trailing-hyphen guard) is copy-pasted verbatim in three separate usecase modules. Each copy differs only in the error type it wraps (`BaselineCaptureError`, `CatalogueImplSignalsError`, `TypeSignalsError`). All three copies even carry the identical doc comment 'Mirrors the domain `TrackId::try_new` validation without importing domain.' Any change to the slug grammar (e.g. allowing dots, banning consecutive digits) must be made in all three files — classic change-amplification.

rationale: Three copies of non-trivial validation logic (slug grammar with multiple distinct rules) live within the same crate. A grammar change requires touching all three files, and a divergence (e.g. one copy accepts uppercase, another does not) would cause inconsistent behaviour between use cases. The copies also triplicate the associated tests. The fix is straightforward: extract a generic helper (e.g. a free function returning `bool` or `Option<&str>` describing the failure reason) into a shared `usecase` internal module and call it from each interactor.
suggested_fix: Extract the core slug-validation predicate into a shared internal module (e.g. `libs/usecase/src/common/track_id_validation.rs`) as `fn validate_track_id_slug(id: &str) -> Result<(), String>` returning the failure reason as a `String`. title: Inline `rev-parse HEAD` pattern for reading current HEAD SHA duplicated 3 times
description: The pattern of calling `git rev-parse HEAD`, checking `output.status.success()`, and trimming stdout to get the current HEAD SHA string is expressed inline three times in production code (outside tests). The `GitRepository` trait already encapsulates git invocation, but there is no trait method for "get HEAD SHA" — callers reach through `git.output(&["rev-parse", "HEAD"])` directly and repeat the success-check + utf8-decode + trim pattern each time.

output rev-parse HEAD via .ok().filter(success).map(trim) for head_hash

rationale: Each caller independently encodes the knowledge that HEAD SHA is obtained via `rev-parse HEAD` and the output must be trimmed. The `GitRepository` trait (in `libs/infrastructure/src/git_cli/mod.rs`) already houses `current_branch()` and `index_tree_hash()` as conveniences — a `head_sha()` method following the same pattern would centralize this rule. A divergence in error handling is already present: `commit_hash.rs` returns a hard error on failure, while `pr/poll.rs` silently treats failure as `None`.
suggested_fix: Add a `head_sha(&self) -> Result<Option<String>, GitError>` method to the `GitRepository` trait that encapsulates `rev-parse HEAD` + success check + trim. Callers choose their error-handling policy (map to None vs propagate error) but stop re-encoding the git invocation.
verify_reason: Confirmed: three separate sites inline the git rev-parse HEAD + success-check + utf8-trim pattern. commit_hash.rs (lines 52-58) returns a hard error on failure. poll.rs (lines 565-570) uses .ok().filter(success).map(trim) returning Option<String>. poll.rs (lines 622-626) repeats the same Option pattern. The GitRepository trait already has convenience methods (current_branch, index_tree_hash) — a head_sha() method would centralize this. The divergence in error handling is already real. Three copies of same knowledge is a genuine medium-severity DRY violation.
